prompts.title: Refactor Java class
prompts.description: Refactor a Java class to follow SOLID principles and clean architecture.
prompts.prompt:
You are a senior Java engineer. Refactor the following Java class to improve readability, maintainability, and adherence to SOLID principles.

Requirements:
- Apply the Single Responsibility Principle: extract distinct concerns into separate classes.
- Use dependency injection (constructor injection) instead of direct instantiation.
- Replace any magic numbers or strings with named constants.
- Ensure all public methods have clear, intention-revealing names.
- Prefer composition over inheritance where appropriate.
- Add Javadoc to every public method.
- If the class violates the Open/Closed Principle, introduce interfaces or abstractions.
- Preserve existing behaviour — this is a refactor, not a feature change.

Original class:
```java
[paste your Java class here]
```

Return the refactored code with a short summary of every change you made and why.
prompts.author: digvijay

prompts.title: Spring Boot REST endpoint
prompts.description: Generate a production-ready Spring Boot REST endpoint with validation and error handling.
prompts.prompt:
You are a senior Spring Boot engineer. Generate a complete, production-ready REST endpoint for the following resource.

Resource: [describe the resource, e.g. "User profile with fields: id, name, email, role"]

Requirements:
- Use Spring Boot 3+ with Jakarta EE annotations.
- Create: Controller, Service interface + implementation, Repository (Spring Data JPA), DTO (request + response), and Entity.
- Controller must use @RestController, proper HTTP methods, and return ResponseEntity.
- Add Bean Validation annotations on the request DTO (@NotBlank, @Email, @Size, etc.).
- Service layer must handle business logic; controller stays thin.
- Add a @ControllerAdvice global exception handler that returns RFC 7807 ProblemDetail.
- Use MapStruct or manual mapping between Entity ↔ DTO.
- Include pagination support on the list endpoint (Pageable).
- Add OpenAPI @Operation annotations on each controller method.
- Follow package structure: controller / service / repository / dto / entity / exception.

Return all files with their full package paths.
prompts.author: digvijay

prompts.title: JUnit 5 unit tests
prompts.description: Generate thorough JUnit 5 unit tests with Mockito following the AAA pattern.
prompts.prompt:
You are a senior Java engineer specializing in testing. Write comprehensive JUnit 5 unit tests for the following class.

Class under test:
```java
[paste your Java class here]
```

Requirements:
- Use JUnit 5 (org.junit.jupiter) and Mockito (@ExtendWith(MockitoExtension.class)).
- Follow the Arrange-Act-Assert (AAA) pattern in every test. Add // Arrange, // Act, // Assert comments.
- Use @Mock for dependencies and @InjectMocks for the class under test.
- Cover: happy path, edge cases, null inputs, empty collections, boundary values, and exception scenarios.
- Use @DisplayName with human-readable test names describing the scenario and expected outcome.
- Use @ParameterizedTest with @CsvSource or @MethodSource where multiple inputs test the same logic.
- Use assertThrows for exception testing; verify the exception message.
- Use Mockito.verify() to assert interactions with mocks where relevant.
- Use @Nested classes to group related test scenarios.
- Aim for ≥ 90% branch coverage.
- Do not test private methods directly — test them through public API.

Return the complete test class, ready to compile and run.
prompts.author: digvijay

prompts.title: Design pattern advice
prompts.description: Get expert advice on which design pattern best solves your specific problem.
prompts.prompt:
You are a senior software architect with deep knowledge of GoF design patterns, enterprise patterns, and modern architectural patterns.

I have the following design problem:
[describe your problem, e.g. "I need to support multiple payment providers (Stripe, PayPal, bank transfer) and easily add new ones without modifying existing code"]

Context:
- Language/framework: [e.g. Java 21 / Spring Boot 3]
- Current architecture: [e.g. layered monolith, microservices]
- Constraints: [e.g. must be backward compatible, high throughput, team is junior]

Please:
1. Recommend the most appropriate design pattern(s). Explain why they fit this specific problem.
2. Show a concrete implementation in [language] with realistic class/method names from my domain.
3. Explain the trade-offs: what do I gain, what complexity does it add, when would this pattern be overkill?
4. If there are common anti-patterns or mistakes when implementing this pattern, warn me.
5. Provide a UML-style text diagram (ASCII or Mermaid) showing the class relationships.
6. Suggest how to test the pattern effectively (what to mock, what to integration-test).

Be opinionated — recommend one primary pattern rather than listing five options.
prompts.author: digvijay
